en.extract:
The Jamaican owl (Asio grammicus) is a medium-sized owl that is endemic to the island of Jamaica.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Jamaican owl was previously considered the only member of the genus Pseudoscops, but phylogenetic analysis has reclassified it within Asio. It is monotypic. 


Description
The Jamaican owl is 27 to 33 cm (11 to 13 in) long; one specimen of unstated sex weighed 335 g (11.8 oz). This medium-sized owl has long ear tufts, dark brown eyes, and a rufous facial disk rimmed in black-flecked white. The adult's upperparts are rufous with fine dark brown vermiculation. The tail is also rufous, with dark brown bars. The breast and belly are rufous with narrow dark brown streaks. The juvenile's back is light grayish brown and the rest of its plumage is light cinnamon-buff.


Distribution and habitat
The Jamaican owl is found throughout the island of Jamaica. It inhabits a variety of open and semi-open landscapes such as open woodland, forest edges, plantations, and gardens. It is primarily a bird of coastal and lowland areas, but can be found as high as 600 m (2,000 ft) of elevation in the mountains.


Behavior


Feeding
The Jamaican owl is nocturnal and eats mainly large insects. Its diet also includes spiders, amphibians, lizards, and rodents. There is one record of its taking a bird, a Greater Antillean grackle (Quiscalus niger).


Breeding
The Jamaican owl's breeding phenology is poorly known. Nesting has been reported between March and October and egg-laying between December and June. It lays a clutch of two eggs in a tree cavity or a concealed tree-fork.


Vocalization

Adult Jamaican owls make a "[h]igh, quivering hoot and guttural growl".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Jamaican owl as being of Least Concern. It is considered widespread and common, but "cutting of forest has probably reduced its range and numbers".


References


External links
Stamps (for Jamaica)
Jamaican Owl photo gallery Archived 2016-03-03 at the Wayback Machine VIREO
Photo-High Res--and Article owlpages

The '''Jamaican owl''' ('''''Asio grammicus''''') is a medium-sized [[owl]] that is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the island of [[Jamaica]].<ref name=IOC11.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.2) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=July 2021 |access-date=July 14, 2021 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Jamaican owl was previously considered the only member of the [[genus]] ''Pseudoscops'', but phylogenetic analysis has reclassified it within ''[[Asio]]''. It is [[monotypic]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Salter|first1=Jessie F|last2=Oliveros|first2=Carl H|last3=Hosner|first3=Peter A|last4=Manthey|first4=Joseph D|last5=Robbins|first5=Mark B|last6=Moyle|first6=Robert G|last7=Brumfield|first7=Robb T|last8=Faircloth|first8=Brant C|date=2019-12-12|title=Extensive paraphyly in the typical owl family (Strigidae)|journal=The Auk|volume=137|issue=1|doi=10.1093/auk/ukz070|issn=0004-8038|doi-access=free|hdl=2346/93048|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref name=IOC11.2/> 

== Description ==

The Jamaican owl is {{convert|27|to|33|cm|in|abbr=on}} long; one specimen of unstated sex weighed {{convert|335|g|oz|abbr=on}}. This medium-sized owl has long ear tufts, dark brown eyes, and a rufous facial disk rimmed in black-flecked white. The adult's upperparts are rufous with fine dark brown [[vermiculation]]. The tail is also rufous, with dark brown bars. The breast and belly are rufous with narrow dark brown streaks. The juvenile's back is light grayish brown and the rest of its plumage is light cinnamon-buff.<ref name=JAOW-BOW>Olsen, P.D. and J. S. Marks (2021). Jamaican Owl (''Asio grammicus''), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.jamowl1.01.1 retrieved September 7, 2021</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Jamaican owl is found throughout the island of Jamaica. It inhabits a variety of open and semi-open landscapes such as open woodland, forest edges, plantations, and gardens. It is primarily a bird of coastal and lowland areas, but can be found as high as {{convert|600|m|ft|abbr=on}} of elevation in the mountains.<ref name=JAOW-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===

The Jamaican owl is nocturnal and eats mainly large [[insect]]s. Its diet also includes [[spider]]s, [[amphibian]]s, [[lizard]]s, and [[rodent]]s. There is one record of its taking a bird, a Greater Antillean grackle (''Quiscalus niger'').<ref name=JAOW-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The Jamaican owl's breeding [[phenology]] is poorly known. Nesting has been reported between March and October and egg-laying between December and June. It lays a clutch of two eggs in a tree cavity or a concealed tree-fork.<ref name=JAOW-BOW/>

===Vocalization===

{{birdsong|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Pseudoscops-grammicus |species=Jamaican owl}}
Adult Jamaican owls make a "[h]igh, quivering hoot and guttural growl".<ref name=JAOW-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Jamaican owl as being of [[Least Concern]].<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered widespread and common, but "cutting of forest has probably reduced its range and numbers".<ref name=JAOW-BOW/>

zh.extract:
牙買加鴞（學名Pseudoscops grammicus）是牙買加特有的一種貓頭鷹。它們有深褐色斑紋，面盤呈黃色。眼睛淡褐色，耳羽很長，喙呈灰黃色。它們棲息在開放的環境，一般都是在低地之中。它們是夜間活動的，主要吃昆蟲、蜘蛛、兩棲類、蜥蜴、鳥類及齧齒目。它們會在樹穴中生蛋。它們較為普遍，但在某些地區卻受到威脅。
